# survival

(@tab-surv-data-abs) exemplifies a random survival dataset with $n$ observations (rows) and $p$ features.

| $\mathbf{x}_{;1}$ | $\mathbf{x}_{;2}$ | $\mathbf{x}_{;p}$ | $T$ | $\Delta$ | $Y$ | $C$ |
| -- | -- | --- | -- | --| -- | -- |
| $X_{11}$ | $\cdots$ | $X_{1p}$ | $T_1$ | $\Delta_1$ | $Y_1$ | $C_1$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\ddots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $X_{n1}$ | $\cdots$ | $X_{np}$ | $T_n$ | $\Delta_n$ | $Y_n$ | $C_n$ |

: Theoretical time-to-event dataset. $(Y,C)$ are 'hypothetical' as they can never be directly observed. Rows are individual observations, $X$ columns are features, $T$ is observed time-to-event, $\Delta$ is the censoring indicator, and $(Y,C)$ are hypothetical true survival and censoring times. {#tbl-surv-data-abs}



## survival task box

The estimated prediction functional $\hatg$ is fit on training data $\{(\xx_1,t_1,\delta_1),...,(\xx_n,t_n,\delta_n)\} \in \dtrain$ and is considered good if $\EE[L(T^*, \Delta^*, \hatg(X^*))]$ is low, where $(X^*, T^*, \Delta^*) \sim (X, T, \Delta)$ is independent of $(X_1,T_1,\Delta_1),...,(X_n,T_n,\Delta_n)$ and $\hatg$.

## competing risks notation

To introduce the competing risks setting more formally, let $Y$ be the time-to-event as before.
In the competing risks setting, there is now an additional random variable $E\in \{1,\ldots,q\}$ with realizations $e$, which denotes one of $q$ competing events that can occur at event time $Y$.
The survival outcome notation, $(Y, \Delta)$, thus has to be extended to accommodate for the possibility of competing risks.
Previously, we defined the status indicator $\Delta \in \{0,1\}$ for right-censored data.
In the competing risks setting we have $\Delta = I(Y_i \leq C_i) \wedge E_i = e$, such that
$\Delta \in \{0, 1, \ldots, q\}$ where $\Delta = 0$ indicates censoring as before (that is subject remained in state 0 until end of follow up) and $\Delta = e$ means the $e$th event occurred.
Rather than estimating the distribution of event times $\Pr(Y\leq \tau)$, the goal is to estimate the joint distribution of $Y$ and $E$, $\Pr(Y\leq \tau, E=e)$.

## recurrent events



## Recurrent Events {#sec-recurrent-events}

The *recurrent events* setting is defined by the same event type occurring multiple times.
While the recurrent events setting can be viewed as a special case of the multi-state setting (@sec-multi-state), we treat it here separately as it can also be approached as single event data with correlation.
Some examples of recurrent events include recurrence of infectious diseases, episodes of epilepsy, and replacements of machine parts.

@tbl-cgd-data shows data of chronic granolotomous disease (CGD) for two subjects.
Note the start-stop notation of the data ($t_{start}, t_{stop}$ respectively) that indicates the time in which a subject enters the risk set for the k*th* event number.
The column $\delta$ indicates whether the subject experienced the $k$th outbreak or was censored.
For example, Subject 1 (first three rows) experienced the first outbreak (first row) after 219 days, at which time they entered the risk set for event number 2.
The second event (second row) was experienced at 373 days and the subject was then censored for the third event (third row) after 414 days.
This notation is equivalent to a multi-state representation of the recurrent events process, where each event number represents a different state.
In @fig-recurrent-events, right panel, this is refered to as "Clock forward" approach.

| ID| $k$ | $t_{start}$| $t_{stop}$|$\delta$|$gap$|
|--:|---:|---:|------:|--:|-----:|
|  1|1|  0 | 219|   1| 219|
|  1|2|219 | 373|   1| 154|
|  1|3|373 | 414|   0|  41|
|  7|1|  0 | 292|   1| 292|
|  7|2|292 | 364|   0|  72|
: Subset of the `cgd`  [@pkgsurvival] time-to-event dataset.
Rows are individual observation units ($ID$), $k$ is the $k$th event occurrence, $t_{start}$ and $t_{stop}$ mark the beginning and end of the "at risk time" for the $k$th event occurence, $\delta$ is the event indicator and $gap = t_{stop} - t_{start}$. {#tbl-cgd-data}

For recurrent events data without competing (terminal) events, the "Clock reset" approach (@fig-recurrent-events, left panel) can be used.
Instead of representing the data in the start-stop notation, the gap time is calculated instead (see fourth column in @tbl-cgd-data).
In this representation, we only consider how long the subject was at risk for the $k$th event, not at which time (on the calendar time scale).
It has the advantage that standard methods for analysis of single-event data can be used for modeling.
However, the resulting dependency between multiple data points from one subject (at risk for multiple events at the same time) needs to be taken into account in the modeling step.

![Illustration of transitions in the recurrent events setting. Left: "Clock reset" representation of recurrent events. Each time an event occurs, the clock is reset to 0 and we once again wait for occurence of an event (of the same type); Right: "Clock forward" representation. A subject transitions from 1st, to 2nd, etc. to $K$th event occurence](Figures/survival/recurrent-events.png){#fig-recurrent-events fig-alt="Schematic illustration of recurrent events transitions."}

The distribution of event recurrences per subject is often right-skewed in real data sets, which implies that there are only few events past event number $e^*$.
In practice, therefore, the data is often summarized such that we have event numbers $e = 1,\ldots, e^*_+$, where $e^*_+$ represents category "$e^*$ or higher".

## transition probabilities

Increasing the number of intervals to infinity or equivalently, reducing the interval size to infinitesimally small intervals $du$ where only one transition can be observed, leads to a product integral over the instantaneous transition probabilities $p_{\ell e}(u)$ (@eq-ms-instantaneous-trans-prob), such that
<!--  -->
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P}(\zeta, \tau) & = \lim_{\du \rightarrow 0}\prod_{u \in (\zeta, \tau]}
  \begin{pmatrix}
    p_{00}(u) & \cdots & p_{0q}(u)\\
    \vdots                  & \ddots & \vdots\\
    p_{q0}(u)  & \cdots & p_{qq}(u)\\
  \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-ms-prodint}
<!--  -->



## parametric cause-specific CR


<!-- FIXME: the below is a bit weird here, because it applies to any parametric model, not only PH -->
Instead of using the Cox model, one could theoretically assume a parametric form for the baseline hazard and create a fully-parametric cause-specific model, where again the likelihood is calculated for each independent cause separately:

$$
\calL(\bstheta_e) = \prod^n_{i=1} h_e(t_i|\xx_i, \bstheta_e)^{\delta_{i;e}} S_e(t_i|\xx_i, \bstheta_e)
$$

where $\delta_{i;e}$ is the event indicator for cause $e$.
Note that here $S_e(t_i|\xx_i, \bstheta_e) = \exp(-H_e(t_i|\xx_i, \bstheta_e))$ is the survival for event $e$, not the all-cause survival, and thus assumes that events from other causes are treated as censored.
Once $\bstheta_e$ is estimated, for each cause, the hazard functions $h_e(\tau|\xx, \bstheta_e)$ are immediately available from the definition of the assumed distribution (for example the Weibull distribution) and estimates of the all-cause survival probability and CIF could be once again obtained using the relationships given in @sec-competing-risks.

In practice, this parametric approach doesn't appear to be common, partly because in many applications assuming the same distribution for all causes may not be realistic and data-driven selection of different distributions for different causes would induce additional complexity.



<!-- FIXME: add back in into partiotion based reduction chapter -->



As mentioned before, the choice of interval boundaries $\mathcal{A}$ is important in context of partition based reductions. 
This choice is also closely related to the choice of model class used for estimation (as will be illustrated below).

A general trade-off is between the flexibility and the robustness of the interval-specific hazard estimates. 
Larger number of intervals $J$ leads to more flexible hazard estimation but also potentially to small intervals with only few observations or events. 
This can lead to large differences in hazard estimates between neighboring intervals (which is implausible in many use cases) and sensitivity to the placement of interval boundaries $\mathcal{A}$.
This is illustrated in Figure @fig-pem-interval-comparison.

![Comparison of piecewise exponential model (PEM) hazard estimation with different numbers of intervals $J$ and different placements of interval boundaries $\mathcal{A}$. The figure shows the true underlying hazard function (solid black line) together with PEM estimates using different configurations: (a) few intervals with equidistant boundaries, (b) many intervals with equidistant boundaries, (c) few intervals with data-driven boundaries, and (d) many intervals with data-driven boundaries. This illustrates the trade-off between flexibility and robustness, as well as the sensitivity to the choice of interval boundaries.](Figures/reductions/pem-interval-comparison.png){#fig-pem-interval-comparison fig-alt="Comparison of PEM hazard estimates with different interval configurations against the true underlying hazard function." width=100%}

Therefore, the number and placement of interval boundaries can be viewed as a hyperparameter in the estimation process. 
However the search space quickly becomes large, expensive to explore and doesn't necessarily solve the problem of large differences in neighboring (discrete) baseline hazards. 

A more prorgrammatic approach is to use a sufficiently large number of intervals in order to allow enough flexibility and then use some form of regularization that smooths the baseline hazard estimates. Examples ... and ... present examples of such approaches. 


### Example: Penalized spline regression {#sec-pam}

... show estimtion of PAM and compare to PEM for different number of intervals. Mention big GAM methods by wood, runtime, ...

mention limitation: baseline hazard is assumed to be smooth over entire follow-up. will oversmooth if there are break points. 





### Example: Gradient boosted trees

Recall from @sec-surv-ml-models-boost-regr the definition of boosting. Such models can be used to estimate the GAM model from @sec-pam using univariate GAM models as base learners (reference mboost and friends XXX). However, we can also use trees as base learners, which is often preferred, especially in combination with the popular XGBoost implementation (@cheng2016xgboost).
(Cite Bender 2021 XXX)

Once again, the variable representing time $t_j$ is included as numeric variable in the model. A split w.r.t. to $t_j$ implies a change in the hazard function before and after the split. Interactions between other features and $t_j$ are also learned automatically and allow time-varying effects and non-proportional hazards. 

To apply gradient boosting to the piecewise exponential model, we need to specify the loss function and derive the corresponding gradients (pseudo-residuals) for the Poisson regression model with offset.

Recall from @eq-pem-model that the model specification is
$$\log(\mathbb{E}[\Delta_{ij}]) = \log(h_{ij}) + \log(t_{ij}) = g(\xx_i, t_j) + \log(t_{ij}),$$
where $g(\xx_i, t_j)$ is the function to be learned and $\log(t_{ij})$ is the offset term.

The negative log-likelihood for the Poisson model with offset is given by
$$\begin{aligned}
L(\delta_{ij}, g(\xx_i, t_j)) 
& = -\log \Pr(\Delta_{ij} = \delta_{ij})\\
& = -\left[\delta_{ij}\log(\mu_{ij}) - \mu_{ij} - \log(\delta_{ij}!)\right]\\
& = -\left[\delta_{ij}(\log(h_{ij}) + \log(t_{ij})) - h_{ij}t_{ij} - \log(\delta_{ij}!)\right]\\
& = -\left[\delta_{ij}g(\xx_i, t_j) + \delta_{ij}\log(t_{ij}) - \exp(g(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij} - \log(\delta_{ij}!)\right],
\end{aligned}$$
where $\mu_{ij} = \exp(g(\xx_i, t_j) + \log(t_{ij})) = \exp(g(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij}$ is the expected number of events and the term $\log(\delta_{ij}!)$ is constant with respect to the parameters and can be ignored for optimization purposes.

The gradient of the loss with respect to $g(\xx_i, t_j)$ is
$$\frac{\partial L(\delta_{ij}, g(\xx_i, t_j))}{\partial g(\xx_i, t_j)} = -\delta_{ij} + \exp(g(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij} = -\delta_{ij} + \mu_{ij}.$$

Therefore, the negative gradient (pseudo-residual) at iteration $m$ for observation $(i,j)$ is
$$r_{ijm} = -\frac{\partial L(\delta_{ij}, g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j))}{\partial g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j)} = \delta_{ij} - \exp(g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij} = \delta_{ij} - \hat{\mu}_{ij}^{(m-1)},$${#eq-poisson-pseudo-residual}
where $g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j)$ is the current estimate of the log-hazard from the previous $m-1$ iterations and $\hat{\mu}_{ij}^{(m-1)} = \exp(g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij}$ is the predicted expected number of events.

The pseudo-residual @eq-poisson-pseudo-residual represents the difference between the observed event indicator $\delta_{ij}$ and the predicted expected number of events $\hat{\mu}_{ij}^{(m-1)}$, which is the natural residual for Poisson regression.

**Initial guess:** The initial guess $g_0(\xx_i, t_j)$ is typically set to a constant value. A common choice is to set $g_0(\xx_i, t_j) = \log(\bar{h})$, where $\bar{h}$ is the overall average hazard function, estimated as
$$\bar{h} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^{J_i} \delta_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^{J_i} t_{ij}},$$
which is the total number of events divided by the total time at risk. Alternatively, one can simply initialize $g_0(\xx_i, t_j) = 0$ for all observations, which corresponds to an initial hazard function of $h_0 = 1$.

**Update step:** At each iteration $m$, a regression tree $h_m$ is fitted to the pseudo-residuals $r_{ijm}$ using the features $(\xx_i, t_j)$ as predictors. The tree partitions the feature space and assigns a constant value to each leaf node. The model is then updated as
$$g_m(\xx_i, t_j) = g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j) + \nu h_m(\xx_i, t_j),$${#eq-poisson-boost-update}
where $\nu \in (0,1]$ is the shrinkage parameter (learning rate) that controls the step size of each update.

The complete gradient boosting algorithm for Poisson regression with offset using tree base learners is given in Algorithm @alg-poisson-boost.

**Algorithm:** Gradient Boosting for Poisson Regression with Offset {#alg-poisson-boost}

1. **Initialize:** Set $g_0(\xx_i, t_j) = \log(\bar{h})$ for all $(i,j)$, where $\bar{h} = \frac{\sum_{i,j} \delta_{ij}}{\sum_{i,j} t_{ij}}$, or alternatively $g_0(\xx_i, t_j) = 0$.

2. **For** $m = 1, \ldots, M$:
   1. **Compute pseudo-residuals:** For each observation $(i,j)$ in the training data $\calD_{\mathcal{A}}$:
      $$r_{ijm} = \delta_{ij} - \exp(g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij}$$
   2. **Fit base learner:** Fit a regression tree $h_m$ to the pseudo-residuals:
      $$h_m = \arg\min_{h} \sum_{(i,j) \in \calD_{\mathcal{A}}} (r_{ijm} - h(\xx_i, t_j))^2$$
      The tree partitions the feature space based on $(\xx_i, t_j)$ and assigns constant values to each leaf node.
   3. **Update model:** 
      $$g_m(\xx_i, t_j) = g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j) + \nu h_m(\xx_i, t_j)$$
      where $\nu$ is the shrinkage parameter.

3. **Return:** Final model $\hat{g}(\xx_i, t_j) = g_M(\xx_i, t_j)$.

**Prediction:** For new observations with features $\xx^*$ and time $t_j^*$, the predicted log-hazard is $\hat{g}(\xx^*, t_j^*) = g_M(\xx^*, t_j^*)$, and the predicted hazard function is $\hat{h}(\xx^*, t_j^*) = \exp(\hat{g}(\xx^*, t_j^*))$.

The algorithm iteratively improves the model by fitting trees to the residuals from the previous iteration. Each tree captures patterns in the data that were not well explained by the previous model, and the shrinkage parameter $\nu$ ensures that updates are gradual, reducing overfitting. The offset term $\log(t_{ij})$ is implicitly accounted for in the pseudo-residual calculation, as the predicted expected number of events $\hat{\mu}_{ij}^{(m-1)} = \exp(g_{m-1}(\xx_i, t_j))t_{ij}$ includes the time at risk $t_{ij}$. 

## Conclusion

:::: {.callout-warning icon=false}

## Key takeaways

* The pseudo-value based approach allows to estimate arbitrary quantities of interest conditional on covariates, as long as an univariate, unbiased estimator of the quantity of interest is available.
* It avoids strong assumptions like the proportional hazards assumption or the accelerated failure time assumption, which can be violated in practice.
* In context of machine learning, this approach can greatly simplify analyis of time-to-event data, as standard implementations can be used out-of-the-box without any additional modifications.
* In contrast to many survival specific machine learning methods currently available, it can be applied used in complex settings, including competing risks and multi-state models.
* Feature effects can ben interpreted directly as effects on the quantity of interest, rather than being expressed in terms of hazard ratios or other relative measures.
::::

:::: {.callout-important icon=false}

## Limitations
* Pseudo-values can take negative and positive values, thus direct applicatoin of regression algorithms might produce predictions outside the range of the target. Some methods, like deep learning, support the use of activation functions and custom loss functions, but other learners might need additional adaptation in order to map the predictions to the target space.
* Interpretation of feature effects is only direct, if the response function is the identity function.
* Calculating pseudo-values can be computationally expensive if predictions are needed at many time points $\tau$ and data set size $n$ is large.

::::

:::: {.callout-tip icon=false}

## Further reading

* @Andersen2010 provides an overview of the pseudo-value approach and its application to various quantities of interest in survival analysis.
* @johansen2021regressionmodels propose an extension of pseudo-value based regression for interval censored data using parametric univariate estimators.
* @mogensen2013randomforestb use pseudo-values in combination with random forests for competing risks analysis.
* @Zhao2020 propose a deep learning based approach for survival analysis using pseudo-values.

::::



### Example: Pooled Logistic Regression

When the learner used to estimate the probabilities for an event at time $t_{(j)}$ is a logistic regression model, the method is known as pooled logistic regression (@dagostinoRelationPooledLogistic1990a). Pooled because rather than fitting one separate logistic regression model for each event time $t_{(j)}$, a single logistic regression model is fitted to the stacked data set, conditional on the interval information $t_j$ and potentially other features $\xx_{i}$.




### Example: ICU Competing Risks {#sec-cr-example}

We illustrate the reduction framework using the `sir.adm` dataset [@pkgmvna], previously introduced in @sec-competing-risks.
The data contain 747 patients admitted to an intensive care unit (ICU) with three covariates: pneumonia status at admission (`pneu`), `age`, and `sex`.
The competing events are discharge alive from the ICU ($e=1$) and death in the ICU ($e=2$), with 657 discharges, 76 deaths, and 14 censored observations (@tbl-surv-data-sir.adm).

#### PAM approach

Using the piecewise-exponential additive model (PAM) framework (@sec-piecewise-constant-hazards, @pkgpammtools), the reduction is carried out explicitly:

1. Two cause-specific datasets are created by defining $\delta_{i,1} = \II(e_i = 1)$ (discharge) and $\delta_{i,2} = \II(e_i = 2)$ (death).
Each dataset is then transformed to the piecewise exponential data (PED) format using `as_ped()`.
2. A PAM is fit to each PED using `mgcv::gam()` with a Poisson likelihood:
    - Discharge: `ped_status ~ s(tend) + pneu + s(age) + sex`
    - Death: `ped_status ~ s(tend) + pneu + s(age) + sex`
3. The cause-specific hazard estimates are combined to obtain the all-cause survival probability (@eq-cr-allcause-surv) and the CIFs (@eq-cr-cif-estimate).

@fig-cif-pam-sir shows the resulting CIF estimates for four covariate profiles defined by pneumonia status (yes/no) and age (40 vs. 75 years, male).
As expected, patients with pneumonia and older patients have a higher cumulative incidence of death and a lower cumulative incidence of discharge.
Note how the CIFs for discharge and death sum to approximately one at later time points (reflecting the fact that nearly all patients eventually experienced one of the two events).

![PAM-based CIF estimates for the `sir.adm` data, stratified by four covariate profiles (pneumonia yes/no, age 40/75, male). Left panel: cumulative incidence of death. Right panel: cumulative incidence of discharge.](Figures/reductions/cif-pam-sir.png){#fig-cif-pam-sir fig-alt="PAM-based cumulative incidence function estimates for competing risks of discharge and death in ICU patients, showing higher death incidence for pneumonia and older age profiles." width=100%}


#### Random survival forest approach

Random survival forests (RSF) can handle competing risks natively.
The implementation in the `randomForestSRC` package [@pkgrfsrc] constructs cause-specific cumulative hazard functions internally and combines them to produce CIF estimates.
Thus, while the reduction steps are identical in principle, they are performed within the algorithm rather than by the analyst.

@fig-cif-rfsrc-sir shows the RSF-based CIF estimates for the same four covariate profiles.
The overall patterns are consistent with the PAM results, though the RSF estimates exhibit the characteristic step-function shape of tree-based methods.

![RSF-based CIF estimates for the `sir.adm` data, stratified by four covariate profiles (pneumonia yes/no, age 40/75, male). Left panel: cumulative incidence of death. Right panel: cumulative incidence of discharge.](Figures/reductions/cif-rfsrc-sir.png){#fig-cif-rfsrc-sir fig-alt="Random survival forest-based cumulative incidence function estimates for competing risks of discharge and death in ICU patients." width=100%}


#### Comparison

@fig-cif-comparison-sir overlays the PAM and RSF estimates.
Despite the fundamentally different modeling approaches --- smooth semi-parametric hazards (PAM) versus ensemble-based non-parametric hazards (RSF) --- the CIF estimates are broadly similar for all four profiles.
This provides reassurance that both methods successfully capture the underlying competing risks structure, and highlights the modularity of the reduction approach: the choice of base learner for the cause-specific hazard models can be varied independently from the combination step.

![Comparison of PAM-based (solid lines) and RSF-based (dashed lines) CIF estimates for the `sir.adm` data. Left panel: cumulative incidence of death. Right panel: cumulative incidence of discharge.](Figures/reductions/cif-comparison-sir.png){#fig-cif-comparison-sir fig-alt="Side-by-side comparison of PAM and random survival forest CIF estimates for competing risks in ICU patients, showing broadly similar estimates across methods." width=100%}


### Discussion {#sec-cr-discussion}

Several modeling choices arise within this reduction framework.

**Separate vs. joint models.**
The approach above fits fully separate models for each cause.
An alternative is to fit a single *stacked* model that includes cause-by-covariate interactions, which allows sharing of information across causes (for example, a common baseline hazard structure or shared covariate effects).
This can be advantageous when certain causes have few events, as the shared structure acts as a form of regularization.
In partition-based reductions, interactions between a cause variable and the time variable $t_j$ will yield different baseline hazards for each cause.


**Flexibility in learner choice.**
Because the reduction treats each cause independently, the learner for each cause-specific model does not have to be the same.
For instance, one might use a gradient boosting for cause $1$ and a random forest for cause $2$ (where complex interactions are suspected). This is however somewhat impractical as it introduces additional overhead when merging predictions from two different models.

<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- Moved here from P1C6_survtsk.qmd (Survival Task) on 2026-05-14 -->
<!-- Original commit: a3d987f (Raphael, "refactor conclusion, closes #18", 2026-03-27) -->
<!-- Reasons for move:                                              -->
<!--   1. The factual claim about the `hdfail` dataset is wrong:    -->
<!--      the actual data has integer days (1-11295), not 3-decimal -->
<!--      precision, and 1,292 (not 2,641) unique event times.      -->
<!--   2. The callout argues that prediction at every event time is -->
<!--      unnecessary -- but most software can predict at any τ once-->
<!--      fitted, so the framing should be about TRAINING cost, not -->
<!--      prediction (cf. RCLL, IBS that need predictions exactly at-->
<!--      observed event times).                                    -->
<!-- Possible reuse: the "coarsen time grid for estimation" point   -->
<!-- could be salvaged for a future section on training-cost trade- -->
<!-- offs in non- and semi-parametric models, with a corrected data -->
<!-- example (candidates: `riskRegression::Paquid`, `mets::prt`,    -->
<!-- `mstate::ebmt2`).                                              -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

::: {.callout-note}

# Time points for prediction

In practice, most software implementations produce survival predictions over a discrete set of time points.
These time points are often chosen to coincide with the observed event times in the training data.
This approach is often sensible, particularly when outcomes are recorded on a coarse scale, for example as years until an event occurs.
However, in other datasets this can lead to substantial computational burden without a practical benefit.
For example, the `hdfail` dataset [@Monaco2018] records the time at which hard drives fail with precision to three decimal places, resulting in 2,641 unique event times.
This resolution level is unnecessary for prediction tasks (unless in some very specialist use cases).

Several strategies could be considered to address this issue.
During pre-processing, the time scale could be coarsened, for example converting measurements from hours to days or years, or precision could be reduced through rounding.
Alternatively, predictions could be restricted to a selection of time points, reducing computational burden while preserving the underlying information.

<!-- FIXME: ANDREAS CAN YOU CONFIRM ORIGINAL TIME SCALE IS HOURS? -->

:::


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- Moved here from P4C23_eha_reduction.qmd (Reductions for EHA)  -->
<!-- on 2026-05-15. Originally section "Why a naive 1 - Ŝ_e is    -->
<!-- wrong" (anchor sec-cr-naive-warning) inside §Competing Risks  -->
<!-- via Cause-Specific Hazards. The point is correct but probably -->
<!-- belongs elsewhere (the same caveat is made empirically in     -->
<!-- @sec-cens-vs-cr of the EHA chapter), so parked here.          -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

### Why a naive $1 - \hatS_e$ is wrong {#sec-cr-naive-warning}

It might seem strange to combine cause-specific estimates into an all-cause estimate (Step 3) and then back to cause-specific ones (Step 4).
The CIF is the probability of observing event $e$ by time $\tau$ in the presence of all competing events (@eq-cr-cif-recall), and its computation requires the all-cause survival probability $\hatS(\tau\mid\xx)$, which depends on *all* cause-specific hazards.
Trying to estimate the CIF by fitting a single-event model for cause $e$ and reading off $1 - \hatS_e(\tau\mid\xx) = 1 - \exp(-\hatH_e(\tau\mid\xx))$ does *not* yield a valid CIF.
The resulting quantity, sometimes called the *net probability*, represents the marginal probability of event $e$ in a hypothetical world where the other causes can never occur, and typically overestimates the true CIF.
A direct empirical demonstration on the same data (with the cause-specific Cox model used as the single-event learner) is given in @sec-cens-vs-cr.


<!-- ============================================================ -->
<!-- Below was the application sub-subsection demonstrating both  -->
<!-- marginal and conditional state-occupation probabilities on   -->
<!-- the prothr cohort, originally appended to "### Application   -->
<!-- to liver cirrhosis patients" in P1C5_eha.qmd. Parked here    -->
<!-- because the marginal SOP example raised more interpretation  -->
<!-- questions than it resolved (no uncertainty bands, no         -->
<!-- covariate adjustment, cohort-level prevalence is hard to     -->
<!-- read as either causal or prognostic) and the conditional     -->
<!-- view is numerically identical to the pairwise TPs already    -->
<!-- shown in @fig-multi-state-prothr.                            -->
<!--                                                              -->
<!-- Supporting assets remain in the repo and can be re-spliced:  -->
<!--   book/experiments/prothr_state_occupation.R                 -->
<!--   book/experiments/prothr_state_occupation_by_entry.R        -->
<!--   book/Figures/survival/prothr-state-occupation.png          -->
<!--   book/Figures/survival/prothr-state-occupation-by-entry.png -->
<!-- ============================================================ -->

#### Application: state occupation in the `prothr` cohort

For the `prothr` cohort we now contrast the pairwise transition picture above with the marginal state-occupation view obtained from the same Aalen-Johansen pipeline.
Recall that the transition probabilities $\hatP_{\ell e}(0,\tau)$ shown in @fig-multi-state-prothr are *conditional*: each curve fixes a reference entry state $\ell\in\{0,1\}$ at $\tau = 0$ and reports the probability of being in state $e$ at $\tau$ for that sub-cohort.
Because the `prothr` cohort contains substantial numbers of patients entering in *both* transient states (the empirical entry distribution is approximately $\hat\pi_0 \approx 0.45$ normal, $\hat\pi_1 \approx 0.55$ abnormal across treatment arms), the more informative population-level summary is the marginal state-occupation probability $\hatP_q(\tau)$ from @eq-ms-state-occupation-est, which mixes the two reference entry states according to their empirical weights.

![Estimated state-occupation probabilities $\hatP_q(\tau)$ for the liver cirrhosis cohort, stratified by treatment arm. Bands are stacked so that at every $\tau$ they sum to one: the green band at the bottom is the probability of occupying state $0$ (normal prothrombin), the blue band is state $1$ (abnormal prothrombin), and the red band on top is the absorbing state $2$ (death). The Aalen-Johansen plug-in @eq-ms-aj-trans-prob is computed separately within each arm and then marginalised over the empirical entry-state distribution $\hat\pi_\ell = n_\ell^{(0)}/n$ as in @eq-ms-state-occupation-est.](Figures/survival/prothr-state-occupation.png){#fig-prothr-state-occupation fig-alt="Stacked area plot with two panels (Placebo on the left, Prednisone on the right). The horizontal axis is follow-up time in days from 0 to about 4000, the vertical axis is probability from 0 to 1. Within each panel three colour bands stack to a total height of one: a green band at the bottom for state 0 (normal prothrombin), a blue band above it for state 1 (abnormal prothrombin), and a red band on top for state 2 (death). The red death band grows monotonically with time in both arms, reaching roughly 0.55 at 1825 days for placebo and roughly 0.49 for prednisone. The green normal-prothrombin band widens early before contracting as mortality accumulates; the blue abnormal band shrinks gradually over follow-up."}

@fig-prothr-state-occupation tells a different and complementary story to the pairwise transition probabilities.
At each $\tau$ the three bands partition the cohort exhaustively: the red band on top is the cumulative probability of having reached the absorbing state, $\hatP_2(\tau)$, and its width is precisely the total mortality experienced by time $\tau$, regardless of how patients reached it.
Reading off the figure at $\tau = 1825$ days (five years), roughly $56\%$ of the placebo arm and $49\%$ of the prednisone arm have died, the remaining mass being distributed between the two transient states.
Among the survivors, the green band (state $0$, normal prothrombin) is consistently wider than the blue band (state $1$, abnormal prothrombin) under prednisone, whereas in the placebo arm the two transient bands are closer to parity over much of follow-up — consistent with a treatment-induced shift in steady-state prothrombin status among the still-alive cohort.

It is worth contrasting what the two presentations make visible.
The pairwise transition probabilities in @fig-multi-state-prothr expose the *flow* dynamics conditional on a fixed reference entry state and are interpretable only under the Markov assumption.
The state-occupation curves in @fig-prothr-state-occupation, by contrast, summarise the cohort-level *prevalence* of each state at $\tau$ and, crucially, remain a consistent target of the Aalen-Johansen plug-in even if the Markov assumption fails [@putter2018; @niessl2023].
For descriptive reporting and for prediction questions of the form "what fraction of treated patients will be alive in normal-prothrombin status at five years?", $\hatP_q(\tau)$ is the more direct quantity; the conditional $\hatP_{\ell e}(0,\tau)$ remain useful when the scientific question is explicitly about transitions from a specified starting state.

If we are willing to assume Markovianity, we can refine the picture further by stratifying the state-occupation view by entry state, that is, by re-expressing each row of the Aalen-Johansen transition-probability matrix as a stacked area summing to one (@fig-prothr-state-occupation-by-entry).
This re-uses exactly the numbers underlying @fig-multi-state-prothr; only the presentation differs, with each row of the matrix collapsed into a single panel that shows how a sub-cohort with a fixed entry state $\ell$ redistributes across the three states over time.

![Conditional state-occupation probabilities $\hatP_{\ell q}(0,\tau)$ for the liver cirrhosis cohort, by entry state (rows) and treatment arm (columns). Within each panel the three bands stack to one and represent the same row $\ell$ of the Aalen-Johansen transition-probability matrix shown line-by-line in @fig-multi-state-prothr. Top row: sub-cohort entering in state $0$ (normal prothrombin); bottom row: sub-cohort entering in state $1$ (abnormal prothrombin). Consistency of these conditional curves relies on the Markov assumption.](Figures/survival/prothr-state-occupation-by-entry.png){#fig-prothr-state-occupation-by-entry fig-alt="Two-by-two grid of stacked-area plots. Rows correspond to entry state (top: normal; bottom: abnormal); columns to treatment arm (left: placebo; right: prednisone). Horizontal axis is follow-up time in days from 0 to 4000; vertical axis is probability from 0 to 1. Each panel shows three coloured bands summing to one: a green band for state 0 (normal prothrombin), a blue band for state 1 (abnormal prothrombin), and a red band on top for state 2 (death). In the top row the red death band climbs from 0 to roughly 0.5 over follow-up while the green band gradually narrows; the blue band is thin throughout. In the bottom row (entry state abnormal) the green band rises quickly from 0 to about 0.45 within the first year before contracting; the blue band starts at 1 and shrinks; the red band grows faster than in the top row, reaching about 0.6 by 1825 days in the placebo arm and about 0.55 in the prednisone arm."}

The bottom row of @fig-prothr-state-occupation-by-entry exposes a clinically relevant dynamic that the marginal view averages out: among patients entering with abnormal prothrombin, the probability of having normalised within the first year is substantial (the green band climbs from $0$ to roughly $0.45$ before mortality starts to dominate), and prednisone shifts this redistribution further toward normalisation than placebo does.
Numerically, $\hatP_{1,0}(0,\,1825) \approx 0.29$ under placebo versus $\approx 0.35$ under prednisone, with a corresponding gap in five-year mortality ($\hatP_{1,2}(0,\,1825) \approx 0.60$ vs. $\approx 0.55$).
For patients entering in state $0$ (top row), mortality at five years is markedly lower in both arms and the blue band remains thin throughout, reflecting that conversions to the abnormal state are short-lived because they tend to be followed promptly by either recovery or death.
## Popular Models {#sec-nnet-popular}

Each of the three strategies developed in @sec-nnet-surv has well-known instantiations in the literature.
The descriptions below cover the most influential models and locate each within the taxonomy already established.

### DeepSurv and Faraggi–Simon (Cox-based) {.unnumbered}

The earliest neural-Cox model was the single-hidden-layer network of @Faraggi1995, in which the linear predictor of the Cox model was replaced by a small neural network and the partial likelihood was used as the training objective.
*DeepSurv* [@Katzman2018] extended this to deep multi-layer perceptrons, added dropout and weight decay for regularization, and demonstrated competitive performance on a number of benchmark datasets.
Implementations exist in the `pycox` Python package [@pkgpycox] and in major deep-learning frameworks [@pkgkeras; @pkgtensorflow].
DeepSurv is, in essence, the natural deep-learning analogue of the Cox model (@sec-classical-cox) and is the canonical example of the Cox-based strategy of @sec-nnet-cox.

### Cox-Time (Cox-based, non-proportional) {.unnumbered}

*Cox-Time* [@Kvamme2019a] relaxes the proportional-hazards assumption by allowing the network to take time as an additional input, $g(\xx_i, t; \bstheta)$, so that the relative risk score can vary with time.
The resulting hazard $h(t \mid \xx) = h_0(t)\exp\bigl(g(\xx, t; \bstheta)\bigr)$ accommodates non-proportional hazard patterns; a modified loss replaces the exact risk set with a subset sampled from the mini-batch, which keeps training mini-batchable.
Cox-Time is the most popular Cox-based deep model to support time-varying effects.

### DeepHit (discrete-time, competing risks) {.unnumbered}

*DeepHit* [@Lee2018a] is the most widely used discrete-time survival network.
It parametrizes the joint distribution over event times and causes as a multinomial output over $J \cdot Q + 1$ categories (one for each $(j, q)$ pair plus a "no event by end of follow-up" category) using a softmax activation.
The loss augments the discrete-time log-likelihood of @eq-nnet-discrete-loss with a ranking term that directly optimizes concordance,
$$
L_{\text{rank}}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \sum_{i \neq j} \delta_i \,\II(t_i < t_j)\,\sigma\!\left(\frac{\hatS(t_i \mid \xx_i) - \hatS(t_i \mid \xx_j)}{\omega}\right)
$$
for a bandwidth parameter $\omega > 0$.
The total loss is a convex combination $\alpha\,(-\ell^d) + (1-\alpha)\,L_{\text{rank}}$, where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is a hyper-parameter.
DeepHit was originally designed for competing risks via the shared-trunk pattern of @sec-nnet-cr and remains a standard baseline whenever competing risks are present.

### Nnet-survival (discrete-time) {.unnumbered}

*Nnet-survival* [@Gensheimer2019] is a minimalist discrete-time network: the output layer has $J$ sigmoidal units producing the discrete hazards $\hat{h}^d(j \mid \xx)$, and the loss is the plain discrete-time log-likelihood of @eq-nnet-discrete-loss.
Without the ranking term of DeepHit and with no restriction on the network architecture, Nnet-survival is the most direct deep-learning analogue of the discrete-time reduction technique (@sec-discrete-time-reduction).
By including the interval index as a feature it can model time-varying covariate effects, and by stacking convolutional or recurrent layers it can also handle images or sequences.

### PC-Hazard (piecewise exponential) {.unnumbered}

*PC-Hazard* [@Kvamme2021] is a piecewise-exponential (PEM) deep model: a single network outputs the log-hazard in each interval of a fixed partition, and training minimizes the Poisson-form PEM negative log-likelihood (cf. @sec-piecewise-constant-hazards).
Unlike DeepHit and Nnet-survival, PC-Hazard preserves the exact event and at-risk times within each interval and therefore avoids the information loss of pure discrete-time methods.

### DeepPAMM (piecewise additive mixed model) {.unnumbered}

*DeepPAMM* [@Kopper2021] is a further generalization of the PEM strategy that combines the structured additive predictor of piecewise-exponential additive mixed models with deep neural-network components.
It supports time-varying effects (by interacting the network output with the interval index), left-truncation (by adjusting the risk-time accumulation in the PEM likelihood), competing risks and multi-state outcomes (via the multi-state interpretation of the PEM), recurrent events (via random effects), and multi-modal inputs (via the additive structured predictor in @eq-multimodal).
It is the most "task-complete" of the deep-survival models in the sense of @Wiegrebe2024 and serves as a useful unifying reference point connecting the deep-learning strategies of this chapter to the partition-based reduction techniques of @sec-partition-based-reductions.
### Non-Parametric: Discrete- and Piecewise-Time Neural Networks {#sec-nnet-discrete}

The third strategy makes no parametric or proportional-hazards assumption and instead discretizes the follow-up period, framing survival estimation as a sequence of interval-wise classification or Poisson-regression problems, as introduced in @sec-partition-based-reductions.

Given a partition $\mathcal{A} = \{a_0, \ldots, a_J\}$ of the follow-up period into $J$ intervals $(a_{j-1}, a_j]$, let $J_i$ be the index of the interval in which observation $i$'s outcome falls.
The *discrete hazard* in interval $j$ conditional on covariates $\xx$ is
$$
h^d(j \mid \xx) = \Pr(T \in (a_{j-1}, a_j] \mid T > a_{j-1}, \xx),
$$
and the corresponding discrete survival function is
$$
S^d(j \mid \xx) = \prod_{k=1}^{j}\bigl(1 - h^d(k \mid \xx)\bigr).
$$

A neural network with $J$ output units and sigmoid activations can predict these $J$ discrete hazards simultaneously:
$$
\left(\hat{h}^d(1 \mid \xx_i), \ldots, \hat{h}^d(J \mid \xx_i)\right) = \sigma\!\left(g(\xx_i; \bstheta)\right) \in (0,1)^J.
$$
The discrete-time log-likelihood (@eq-discrete-likelihood-1) then serves as the training objective:
$$
-\ell^{d}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[\sum_{j=1}^{J_i - 1}\log\bigl(1 - \hat{h}^d(j \mid \xx_i)\bigr) + \delta_i \log \hat{h}^d(J_i \mid \xx_i) + (1-\delta_i)\log\bigl(1 - \hat{h}^d(J_i \mid \xx_i)\bigr)\right].
$$ {#eq-nnet-discrete-loss}

This is precisely the binary cross-entropy loss applied interval-by-interval, which is a standard and well-supported loss in deep learning frameworks.
Once trained, the full predicted survival curve is recovered as the running product $\hatS(\tau \mid \xx^*) = \prod_{j : a_j \leq \tau}\bigl(1 - \hat{h}^d(j \mid \xx^*)\bigr)$.

The number of intervals $J$ and the placement of interval boundaries $\mathcal{A}$ are important modeling choices, as discussed in @sec-choice-of-interval-boundaries.
A fine partition allows more flexible hazard estimation but increases the output dimension and may require more training data; a coarse partition is more parsimonious but may miss important temporal patterns.

A close continuous-time alternative uses the *piecewise exponential model* (PEM, @sec-piecewise-constant-hazards), in which the hazard is assumed constant within each interval but the exact event and at-risk times within the interval are preserved.
The Poisson-form likelihood of the PEM is the training objective and the network output is the log-hazard in each interval rather than a discrete hazard.
This avoids the information loss incurred by replacing the exact event time $t_i$ with the interval index $J_i$ and provides continuous-time predictions $\hatS(\tau \mid \xx^*)$ for any $\tau$ via the piecewise cumulative hazard.

Both variants make no distributional assumptions, can model arbitrary time-varying covariate effects by including the interval index $j$ (or the interval midpoint $a_{j-1/2}$) as an input feature, and are implemented using losses that are standard in all major deep learning frameworks.
They are also the direct deep learning instantiation of the partition-based reduction techniques (@sec-partition-based-reductions): the neural network serves as the (multi-output) classification or Poisson-regression model whose targets are the interval-wise event indicators of the transformed data.

## Strengths of Deep Learning for Survival Analysis (from NN chapter)

The three strategies above already make neural networks competitive with classical and tree-based models on tabular survival data, but they do not yet exhibit a clear comparative advantage [@Wiegrebe2024].
Two settings, however, play to neural networks' specific strengths: settings with multiple competing event types, where a *shared representation* can be learned once and re-used by cause-specific heads, and *multi-modal* data, where modality-specific encoders (CNNs for images, FFNNs for tabular data, etc.) can be plugged in and trained jointly under a single survival objective.

### Competing Risks via Shared Networks

In the competing-risks setting a subject can transition to one of $Q$ mutually exclusive terminal states, with cause-specific hazards $h_q(t \mid \xx)$ for $q = 1,\ldots,Q$.
A naïve neural-network solution is to train $K$ separate single-event networks, one per cause, each ignoring observations whose event is of a different cause (treating them as censored at the time of the competing event).
The richer alternative — and the dominant pattern in the deep-learning–for-survival literature [@Wiegrebe2024] — is to share parameters across causes by stacking a common *feature extractor* (a block of shared hidden layers that maps inputs to a learned representation), followed by $Q$ cause-specific *heads* that produce the cause-specific predictions.

Formally, decompose the network as $g(\xx; \bstheta) = (\psi_1(\phi(\xx)), \ldots, \psi_Q(\phi(\xx)))$, where $\phi$ is the shared feature extractor and $\psi_q$ are the cause-specific heads.
Training is by joint minimization of the sum of cause-specific losses, e.g.
$$
-\ell_{\text{CR}}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain) = \sum_{q=1}^{Q} \left[-\ell^{(q)}(\bstheta \mid \dtrain)\right],
$$
where $\ell^{(q)}$ is the cause-$q$ contribution under one of the three survival strategies (the cause-specific partial likelihood for a Cox-based head, a discrete-time log-likelihood with softmax-over-causes for a discrete-time head, or a parametric sub-distribution log-likelihood).

This shared-representation pattern is an instance of *multi-task learning*: the feature extractor $\phi$ is encouraged to learn representations that are useful for predicting all causes simultaneously, which acts as a form of regularization and typically improves sample efficiency when the same covariates are predictive of multiple event types.

### Multi-modal Data

A second comparative advantage of neural networks is their ability to learn jointly from heterogeneous *modalities*: tabular clinical features, medical images, free text, gene-expression vectors, or wearable-device time series.
Each modality has its own natural architectural prior — convolutional networks for images, transformers for text, FFNNs for tabular features — but all of them produce real-valued embeddings that can be concatenated (or otherwise fused) and fed into a single survival head.

Formally, generalize the linear predictor from a single covariate vector $\xx$ to a tuple $(\xx, \mathbf{z}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{z}_G)$ of modalities and write
$$
\eta_i = f(\xx_i) + \sum_{g=1}^{G} d_g(\mathbf{z}_{ig}),
$$
where $f$ is the tabular encoder and $d_g$ are the modality-specific embedding networks for $G$ unstructured modalities (image, text, omics, …) [@Wiegrebe2024].
